author: Dr. Marcus Hale (Hepatology)
content:
HEPATOLOGY CLINIC NOTE — UNIVERSITY
Date: 2/12/2027

INTERVAL HISTORY: Worsening abdominal distension and dyspnea from recurrent ascites and right hydrothorax. Over the past several weeks I increased her furosemide and spironolactone in an attempt to control the fluid.

DATA (LAB_HX_001): On the higher diuretic doses her creatinine rose to 1.6 (from ~1.1 baseline), potassium climbed to 5.6, and sodium fell to 126. These changes reversed somewhat after pulling the doses back to spironolactone 50 / furosemide 20.

ASSESSMENT/PLAN:
- Diuretic-refractory ascites/hydrothorax with clear DIURETIC INTOLERANCE — higher doses produce azotemia, hyperkalemia, and hyponatremia. We cannot safely diurese her further.
- Therefore, plan to rely on MECHANICAL DRAINAGE (therapeutic paracentesis/thoracentesis) for symptom control rather than more diuretic.
- When symptomatic, she will go to the ED for drainage (and we will explore options closer to home given the 90-minute travel).
- Continue lactulose/rifaximin; continue transplant evaluation; reinforce abstinence.
attestation: Electronically signed by Dr. Marcus Hale, MD (Hepatology). 2/12/2027.
